title: Oeufs en Cocotte
subtitle: French baked eggs in cream — a five-ingredient dish that takes fifteen minutes
description: Oeufs en cocotte — eggs baked in small cocotte pots in a bain-marie — is the simplest and most elegant French egg dish. An egg cracked into a ramekin with a spoonful of cream and a knob of butter, baked until the white is just set and the yolk still runny. The variations are infinite: a spoonful of good ham, wilted spinach, or a slick of tomato sauce on the base. The base recipe is pure.
sourceNotes: Oeufs en cocotte appears in Auguste Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903) and was a standard of early twentieth century French bourgeois cookery. The bain-marie technique ensures the delicate white sets gently without the yolk overcooking.

recipeTools.notes: Two individual ramekins or small cocottes, 150–200 ml capacity each.
Deep enough to hold 2–3 cm of boiling water for the bain-marie.

glossaryTerms.definition: A water bath — a roasting tin filled with hot water in which ramekins or moulds are placed in the oven. The water moderates the heat and ensures delicate dishes (custards, egg preparations) cook gently and evenly.

steps: Heat the oven to 180 °C fan (200 °C / gas 6). Boil a kettle.
Butter the ramekins generously. Add 2 tablespoons of cream to each. Season with salt and pepper.
Crack one egg into each ramekin, taking care not to break the yolk.
Place the ramekins in a roasting tin. Pour enough boiling water into the tin to come halfway up the sides of the ramekins. This is the bain-marie.

Carefully transfer to the oven and bake for 10–12 minutes. The timing depends on how runny you prefer the yolk: 10 minutes gives a liquid yolk, 12 minutes gives a yolk that is just beginning to set. Check by gently shaking the tin — the white should be set but the yolk should wobble.
Remove from the bain-marie immediately — the eggs will continue to cook in the ramekin. Scatter with chives and serve at once with toasted bread for dipping.

content.text: The window between a runny yolk and a set one is very narrow — roughly 2 minutes. Set a timer and check at 10 minutes. The eggs will continue to cook in the hot ramekins for 30–60 seconds after they leave the
, so pull them when they look slightly underdone if you want a runny yolk.

Heat the oven to 180 °C fan. Boil a kettle. Butter the ramekins and add {{double-cream}} of cream divided between them (2 tbsp each). Season. Crack {{egg}} of eggs into the ramekins — one per ramekin. Place in a roasting tin and pour boiling water to halfway up the sides. Bake for 10–12 minutes: 10 for a liquid yolk, 12 for a yolk that is just starting to set. Remove from the bain-marie immediately, scatter with chives, and serve with toast.

Add a spoonful of good cooked ham, a few wilted spinach leaves squeezed dry, a thin layer of ratatouille, or a smear of mushroom duxelles on the base of the ramekin before adding the cream and egg. The technique is identical regardless of what goes underneath.
